articles.title: The Adoptee's Body: Where Preverbal Trauma Lives
articles.body:
TL;DR

Preverbal trauma from adoption doesn't live in your memory. It lives in your body. Somatic practices can release what talk therapy can't reach. Healing means learning to feel safe inside your own skin.

The Body Remembers What the Mind Can’t

You don't remember the moment of separation. You couldn't. You hadn't yet formed the brain circuits for autobiographical memory. But your body remembered everything. The cold of the new hands. The absence of the familiar heartbeat. The smell that wasn't yours. All of it got coded into your nervous system, your fascia, your breath. And it's still there, running the show, decades later.

I've worked with hundreds of adoptees over the years. Across the dozens of articles I've written on adoptee trauma, one truth keeps surfacing: the body holds what the mind can’t touch. Talk therapy alone rarely reaches these depths. You can talk about your feelings, your patterns, your fears. But until you work with the body, you're dealing with the tip of the iceberg. The primal wound is somatic before it’s psychological.

Bessel van der Kolk's research, captured in The Body Keeps the Score (paid link), shows how trauma gets held in the body. For adoptees, the first trauma often arrives before language. That's preverbal. That's the root. And it leaves a mark that no amount of talk can erase. But you can teach your nervous system a new song.

Here's the thing: if you've ever felt like you're on high alert for no reason, or you collapse into exhaustion after social events, or you dissociate when things get too intimate, you're not broken. Your body is doing exactly what it learned to do before you could even speak.

Preverbal Trauma: When Separation Happened Before Words

Think about it. As a newborn, your entire world was your mother's body. Her heartbeat, her voice, her scent, her movements. That was your environment. Then, suddenly, it was gone. Whether it was at birth, at two days, or at two months, the severance was absolute. For many adoptees, especially those in transracial or late-discovery adoptions, additional layers of disconnection followed. The shock never got processed. It's locked in the tissues.

The CDC's research on Adverse Childhood Experiences confirms that early separation and disruption are significant sources of toxic stress. But for adoptees, it often starts preverbally. You can't access it through narrative memory. You can only access it through sensation. That flutter in your chest when someone says goodbye. That knot in your throat when you need to speak up. That urge to flee when things get quiet.

I've seen clients describe a lifelong sense of not belonging, a constant inner alarm, a body that never fully relaxes. That's preverbal. That's the adoptee's baseline. And it's exhausting. That's why many of us end up with chronic fatigue, autoimmune issues, or addictions. The body is trying to cope with a threat it's never been able to name.

If you've struggled with attachment and why relationships feel so complicated, know that your body learned something very early: connection isn't safe. So you approach every person bracing for loss. It's not in your head. It's in your muscles, your gut, your breath.

Why Talk Therapy Often Fails Adoptees

Look, I'm not knocking therapy. I believe in it. But with preverbal trauma, the top-down model has limits. You can analyze your childhood for years without ever calming your hypervigilant nervous system. That's because the amygdala and the insula don't speak English. They speak sensation. They speak breath. They speak movement. Until you address the body directly, the pattern stays put.

Many adoptees report feeling worse after digging into their story without the right somatic container. That's because you're activating the trauma without the resources to discharge it. Your body goes into threat mode, and your prefrontal cortex gets hijacked. All that insight becomes just another story you tell yourself.

Does that land? I've watched adoptees leave traditional therapy feeling more fragmented. Not because they didn't work hard, but because the body was never invited to the conversation. If you're constantly asking, "who am I?" with your mind but ignoring the signals from your chest and gut, you're missing the whole picture. Adoptee identity struggles aren’t just about genealogy. They're about the felt sense of being real, being whole.

Somatic Practices for Adoptee Healing

So what works? The path out is through the body. Slowly. Safely. In tiny, tolerable doses. You don't need to relive the trauma. You need to teach your nervous system that this moment is safe. That you're an adult now. That the old alarms aren't necessary.

I always start with grounding. Something simple. Feel your feet on the floor. Notice the temperature of the air on your skin. Press your hands against a solid surface. It sounds too basic, but for a dysregulated nervous system, it's the first step. When you ground, you give your body a "here" and "now." Preverbal trauma lives in a timeless fog. Grounding punctures that.

Orienting

Next is orienting. Let your eyes wander around the room. Let your neck turn. Notice the colors, the shadows, the sounds. I've seen clients literally startle out of this because they haven't looked around with intent in years. When we're on high alert, we either stare or scan. We don't orient in a relaxed, curious way. Teaching your body to orient without threat is enormous.

Try it right now. Look up from this screen. Notice three things you can see. Then three things you can hear. Notice if your breath changes. That's the start of regulation.

Resourcing

Resourcing means building an internal sense of okayness. It's finding memories, images, or sensations that feel pleasant or neutral. For adoptees, this can be hard. Many of us lack a felt sense of goodness inside. But it's essential. You might recall a moment when you felt held, even if it was by a pet, a tree, a book. Hold onto that sensation for a count of ten. That's it. Over time, you create a new neural pathway. The world isn't always dangerous.

In my practice, I often pair this with addressing the behaviors adoptees use to numb because resourcing is the antidote to numbing. When the body has its own resources, it doesn't need to seek them in substances, food, or endless scrolling.

Tools for Nervous System Regulation

You can't talk your way into safety. But you can stimulate the vagus nerve. You can learn to feel your edges. I'll share a few tools I've seen work miracles.

Weighted blankets aren’t just trendy. The deep pressure activates the parasympathetic nervous system. It's a primal signal: you’re held. For an adoptee, that signal was often missing. The Bearaby Cotton Napper (paid link) is one I recommend. It's cooling, heavy, and it doesn't shift off you during the night.

Sound sensitivity is common among adoptees. The nervous system is already on edge, and noise can push it over. That's why I often suggest earplugs, not to withdraw, but to lower the sensory load while your system learns to handle more. Loop Quiet Ear Plugs (paid link) reduce noise by 27 decibels without completely blocking the world. You can still hear conversation, just softer. They've helped many of my clients with night terrors and social overwhelm.

For those wanting a deeper go into body-based trauma work, Trauma and the Body (paid link) by Pat Ogden is a foundational text. It explains how sensorimotor psychotherapy accesses memories held in the body. It's a dense read, but it'll change how you see yourself.

The Sacred Slowness of Healing

Here's what I want you to take away. Your body has been trying to protect you for a long time. It learned its lessons early and it learned them thoroughly. It's not your enemy. It's a loyal guardian that needs to be told the war is over. That conversation happens in the quiet places: on a yoga mat, in a rocking chair, during a walk where you feel the ground beneath you. Words can signal the new reality, but the body must be shown.

This isn’t about forcing relaxation. It's about inviting. It's about noticing when your jaw is tight and gently letting it soften. It's about noticing when your breathing is shallow and allowing a deeper exhale. Over and over. That's the practice. And it works. On the other side of it, even the most complicated reunion will feel less like a threat and more like an encounter with reality.

You might discover grief during this process. That's okay. Grief is the body's way of releasing what it carried too long. Let it move. You might feel anger. That's okay too. You were hurt before you had words. Your body's rage is ancient and valid. Don't rush to forgive. Don't override. Stay with it. The goal isn’t to bypass. The goal is to feel and stay present. That's how integration happens.

I can't promise you'll never get triggered again. But I can promise that over time, your window of tolerance expands. You'll have more space between stimulus and response. You'll be able to choose instead of react. That's freedom. That's the healing I'm here for.

Reviewed by The Oracle Lover, writer and oracle reader. Last updated 2026-05-01.

I've spent years walking with adoptees through the shadows of preverbal pain. This article draws on ancient wisdom and modern science to guide you home to your body.

articles.title: DNA Testing and Adoption: What 23andMe Can (And Can't) Tell You
articles.body:
DNA testing can shatter an adoptee's world, revealing hidden family lines and triggering identity crises that require more than just genetic data to heal.

23andMe and similar services offer clues but can’t provide the emotional "why" behind relinquishment or the deep relational repair adoptees truly need.

Understanding the limits of these tests is essential to avoid fantasy-based expectations and to approach any discovery with grounded, somatic support.

TL;DR

Spitting into a tube and mailing it off feels simple. It's not. For adoptees, that mailer holds a grenade. You might pull the pin expecting a health report and instead get a half sister you never knew existed. Or a father who doesn't know you're alive. Or the truth that your entire origin story was a lie. Here's the thing, DNA tests don't do tender. They don't prepare you. They don't hold you after. They hand you coordinates and walk away, leaving you to find your way out of the wreckage without a map. In our experience writing about adoptee experience, primal wound, identity, reunion, transracial adoption, late-discovery adoptees, we've seen the fallout from at-home DNA tests firsthand. The data is cold. You’re not. So before you click "order," let's talk about what 23andMe can actually tell you, what it can't, and how to stay whole when the ground opens up.

The Emotional Quicksand Nobody Warns You About

Look, I'm not anti-science. I'm anti-using-science-as-a-band-aid-for-a-soul-wound. And that's what a lot of adoptees do. They think if they can just find their biological relatives, they'll finally feel real. They'll know where their nose came from. They'll have a medical history that doesn't end at "adopted." They'll belong. That fantasy is seductive. It's also dangerous.

What 23andMe gives you is a list. Names, percentages, predicted relationships. It doesn't give you the story. It doesn't tell you why your mother left. It doesn't explain whether your father even knows you exist. It can't promise that the person you match with will want to be found, or that they'll be prepared for the emotional earthquake of your appearance. And it absolutely can’t fill the hole left by the primal wound. That wound, as Nancy Verrier names it, isn't about missing information. It's about missing connection at the most fundamental level. That severance leaves a imprint on your nervous system that no genetic report can touch.

Think about it: you get a match for a close relative, maybe a parent. Your body floods with hope, terror, relief, grief, all at once. Your hands shake. Your breath goes shallow. That's your nervous system blaring an alarm, not just a curiosity. And if that relative doesn't respond, or responds with rejection, your inner child doesn't process it as "bummer, no medical history." They process it as I’ve been abandoned again. I’m unworthy. I’m a mistake. The test says nothing about your worth. But your body will make a story out of the silence, and it won't be a kind one.

What 23andMe Can Tell You (And, More In fact, What It Can't)

Let's get practical. 23andMe can tell you your ancestry composition. It can tell you if you’ve genetic markers for certain diseases. It can connect you with DNA relatives who have also opted into the database. That's it. That's the whole thing. It won't tell you what it was like for your birth mother to carry you, to labor, to hand you to a stranger. It won't tell you if your adoption was ethical or coercive. It won't tell you if your birth father was loving or violent, present or long gone. It won't tell you whether you were wanted.

And here's the kicker: the "health" report? It's entertainment dressed as medicine. MedlinePlus, a trusted government source, notes that direct-to-consumer genetic tests can reveal unexpected family relationships and that the health information is often limited and requires confirmation with a healthcare provider. So you could spend $200, get a risk percentage for late-onset Alzheimer's, and spend years bracing for a diagnosis that never comes, all while the real threat to your wellness is the unprocessed trauma from being severed from your mother at birth. Truth is, genetic bewilderment, a term coined by adoptee rights activists, is a real and destabilizing experience. Knowing half your family tree doesn't resolve it. In fact, sometimes it makes it worse. Suddenly you’ve a name, a face, a Facebook profile, and no access at all to the belonging you need. That's a special kind of cruelty the test box doesn't advertise.

Late Discovery: When the Test Tells You Everything You Knew Was a Lie

Read that phrase again: everything you knew was a lie. This is the nightmare scenario of at-home DNA testing, and it's not even that rare. You take the test for fun, maybe to see if you're really part Neanderthal, and the results show you aren't genetically related to your parents. You're adopted. Surprise. Your entire identity, your medical history, your family stories, your mirroring, your sense of self, all of it was built on a fiction.

Honestly, if that's you, I need you to hear this: you’re not broken. You’re not stupid for not knowing. You’re the victim of a massive, society-wide deception that keeps adoptees in the dark and calls it love. And your reaction, whatever it’s, rage, numbness, denial, terror, is completely appropriate. It’s a sane response to an insane situation. Don't let anyone rush you into gratitude or forgiveness. You just found out your life was a construct. You get to grieve that. You get to be furious about that.

This is where the limits of consumer DNA testing become terrifyingly clear. 23andMe can tell you what you aren't. It can't tell you who you’re. It can't tell you what really happened. It can't hold you while you sob on the bathroom floor. It can't guide you through the identity earthquake that follows. That's human work. That's adoptee-competent therapist work. Which brings me to my next point.

The Body Remembers: Somatic Aftermath of Genetic Discovery

That fallout? It's not just psychological. It lands in the body with the full force of developmental trauma. Your shoulders might hunch, your jaw clench, you might find yourself staring blankly at walls or unable to eat. That's dissociation. That's the freeze response. Your body is doing what it learned to do as an infant: shut down to survive overwhelming threat. Finding out your origins are a lie is an overwhelming threat. Gabor Maté's work on stress and illness reminds us that early separation trauma sets the stage for a body on high alert, and a surprise like this pours gasoline on that fire.

So what do you do when you're shaking and the test results are just staring at you? You get support that understands the nervous system, not just the family tree. Somatic therapies, EMDR, sensorimotor psychotherapy, these aren't luxuries. They're lifelines. But they must be adoption informed. A therapist who doesn't understand the primal wound will pathologize your reaction. They'll try to get your thoughts straight when your body is screaming. That's not helpful. That's retraumatizing. For more on finding the right support, see our guide on how to find an adoption-competent therapist. Don’t settle for a generalist. Your nervous system knows the difference.

And while I'm naming helpers, let's name some resources that can hold you when no person seems able. The Primal Wound: Understanding the Adopted Child (paid link) by Nancy Newton Verrier is a touchstone. If you haven't read it, get it. Now. It will name what you've felt your whole life but couldn't articulate. Follow that with Coming Home to Self: The Adopted Child Grows Up (paid link) for a map through the adult experience. And if the trauma responses have you questioning your own mind, What My Bones Know (paid link) by Stephanie Foo is a raw, honest account of healing complex PTSD that will make you feel less insane. These aren't cures. They're companions. They won't fix you because you aren't broken.

Reunion as Rite of Passage, Not Cure

You got a match. Maybe it's a birth parent. Maybe they're open to contact. Maybe you're imagining the movie scene where you meet and cry and all the missing pieces click into place. I need you to hear me: that movie scene is a lie. Reunion isn’t a cure. It's a beginning. It's a relationship with a stranger who shares your smile and maybe none of your values. It's navigating fogs of grief, resentment, hope, and crushing disappointment. It's realizing that getting answers doesn't mean getting closure. It's learning that your birth mother might still be in so much pain she can't really see you. Or she might be guarded, fragile, unable to give you the maternal energy you need because she's still bleeding from the loss of you three decades ago.

Across the dozens of articles we've published on this site about adoptee experience, primal wound, identity, reunion, transracial adoption, late-discovery adoptees, we've seen fantasy devour real possibility more often than not. That said, reunion can be beautiful. It can be healing. But only if you approach it as a slow, messy, human becoming, not a rescue mission. Get support first. Find a therapist, find an adoptee support group, read the literature. Don’t go in raw. And please, for the love of all that’s holy, don’t do this without a plan for your nervous system. Because that first email, that first phone call, will flood you. You'll need tools to stay present. Breathwork, grounding, intentional movement, journaling. These aren't extras. They're first aid.

When Rejection Comes (And It Often Does)

I can't promise your biological family will welcome you. Some do. Many don't. Many are terrified. They're protecting a life they built without you, perhaps a life built on secrets. You might get a message that says "Please don't contact me," or radio silence. That silence can be more devastating than outright hostility because it leaves you nowhere to land. Your mind will spin stories. You'll wonder what's wrong with you. The answer is nothing. The silence is about their terror, their shame, their inability to face what happened. It isn’t a reflection of your value.

But your body won't believe that at first. You'll feel it in your gut. You'll feel the original abandonment lit up like neon. This is the moment when an adoption-competent therapist is non-negotiable. You need someone who can say, "This isn’t about you. This is their wounding. And your pain is real and it matters." You also need fellow travelers. Online adoptee communities, late-discovery support groups, spaces where you can scream and be believed. The connection between adoption and addiction becomes very relevant here, because the impulse to numb this pain with substances, food, or obsessive searching is powerful. Don't go it alone. Reach out. Your life depends on it.

The Fog of False Hope

Remember the adoption fog? That pervasive denial and compliance many adoptees swim in? Well, a DNA test can blast you right out of it, but only if you're ready to feel what you find. Some adoptees get their results, see the names, and then... do nothing. They freeze. They tell themselves they'll deal with it later. That's the fog reasserting itself. The adoption fog is a survival mechanism. It's the blanket we pull over our heads so we can keep functioning. But once you've seen the truth, the blanket has holes. The cold gets in. You can't unknow what you know without doing violence to your own wholeness.

Here's where I get fierce: if you've uncovered a truth that terrifies you, you must not carry it alone. Secrets make us sick. Research on adverse childhood experiences shows that early trauma, unprocessed, shows up in our bodies as inflammation, autoimmune issues, chronic illness. Keeping a secret about your origins is like taking a slow-acting poison. I'm not telling you to post it on Facebook. I'm telling you to find one safe person and speak it out loud. A therapist, a trusted friend who gets it, a support group, even a journal. Let the words leave your body. That's the first step toward metabolizing the shock. And if you need a guide for that inner work, Adoption Healing... a path to recovery (paid link) by Joe Soll offers practical exercises designed specifically for this kind of integration.

So, Should You Take the Test?

That's not for me to decide. I will say this: don't do it lightly. Don't do it on a whim. Don't do it because your spouse thinks it'd be fun. Do it, if you choose, with a full commitment to your own aftercare. Do it knowing that whatever you find, you’re not your genes. You’re not your relinquishment. You’re not the secrets people kept. You’re a soul who has survived a primal severance and is still here, still breathing, still reaching for truth. That's not weakness. That's relentless, sacred strength.

And if you're reading this because you already took the test and your world just blew up, I want you to put your hand on your chest and feel your heartbeat. Feel the animal body that kept you alive all these years. That body knows something the data never will: you belong here. You belong to you. That’s the foundation. Everything else can be built, slowly, painfully, beautifully on top of it. You don't have to do it alone. There’s a whole community of us who get it, who have been where you’re, and who will walk beside you. Just reach out.

The Irreverent Mystic's Benediction

Look, the universe has a twisted sense of humor, handing us bloodlines we didn't choose and then slapping a barcode on our wrist for science to scan. But here we’re, doing the messy, sacred work of becoming real while the statistics scratch their heads and the family tree wilt. Next time someone tells you that knowing your DNA is all you need, laugh. Loudly. Preferably with a snack. And then get back to the real work: tending the soul that no swab can ever map. You're not a data point. You're a living prayer. Amen.

Reviewed by The Oracle Lover, intuitive teacher with 20+ years of practice. Last updated 2026-05-01.

Over the years, I've held space for countless adoptees navigating the aftershocks of DNA revelations, and I stand in awe of the courage it takes to face not only a test result but the entire edifice of identity itself.

articles.title: Closed vs Open Adoption: The Impact on the Adoptee
articles.body:
Closed adoption seals the original birth certificate, often burying the adoptee's origin story under legal lies. Open adoption allows some contact, but it can still feel like a performance of family without the primal claiming. Neither guarantees wholeness; what matters is the adoptee's right to their own truth.

The Fork in the Road

You arrive at two doors. One is locked, no handle, no keyhole. Behind it lies your original name, the face of the woman who birthed you, a medical history that might one day save your life. The other door stands ajar. Through it you can hear voices, maybe see a silhouette, but the light is dim and the door can swing shut without warning. Which corridor would you choose? If you were placed for adoption, you never got to choose. The system chose for you - a system built on fantasies of blank slates and grateful children. And yet, here you’re, decades later, feeling the weight of that choice in your body. Muscle tension. A chasm in your chest. The unshakable sense that you’re not quite real. Both closed and open adoption shape the adoptee at a cellular level, but they do so in distinct and often devastating ways. Let's walk through each door, not as a policy debate, but as a tender examination of what it means to live inside these stories.

Closed Adoption: The Locked Box

Here's the thing: closed adoption was never designed with you in mind. It was designed to protect adults - adoptive parents from the threat of reclaiming birth mothers, birth mothers from social shame, the state from messy complications. Your original birth certificate was sealed, often replaced with a falsified document listing your adoptive parents as if you'd sprung from their union. In many states, you still can’t access that uncensored record without a court order, even at fifty. Even after reunion. Even after death.

This erasure isn't just bureaucratic - it's existential. When the state tells you your own beginning is a secret, it teaches you that you’re dangerous to know. That your existence is by nature shameful. That you should be grateful for the new story and never ask questions. Nancy Newton Verrier named this rupture The Primal Wound (paid link): the deep, preverbal severing of the mother-child bond that lives in the nervous system long before it can be articulated. In closed adoption, that wound gets wrapped in a second layer - the absence of any narrative to make sense of the loss. There’s no face to grieve, no name to whisper, no explanation for why your body startles at abandonment cues you can't trace.

And so you learn to comply. To smile on Mother's Day even as a phantom mother haunts your periphery. To say, “I never really thought about it,” while spending nights scrolling search engines. As Betty Jean Lifton writes in Journey of the Adopted Self (paid link), the adoptee becomes a “divided self,” split between the persona the adoptive family requires and the forbidden inner truth. That split shows up as people-pleasing, depression, a chronic sense of being a fraud. You become the compliance mask, so polished that even you forget it's not your real face.

Think about it: if you've ever paid a DNA site $99, bought three databases, and cross-referenced birth indexes in a county you're not sure you were born in, you know the hunger. You've felt the adrenaline of a potential match - a fourth cousin twice removed and a labradoodle - that turns out to be nothing but a data mirage. The lengths we go to find a face that looks like ours would make a cold-case detective jealous. And we do it because closed adoption turns identity into a puzzle with half the pieces missing and the box hidden in the attic. It's no wonder so many late-discovery adoptees describe the revelation like a bomb. An earthquake. A death of the only self they knew.

Open Adoption: A Window, Not a Door?

Now, the other path. Open adoption promises something different: contact. Visits. Letters. Maybe even a relationship with the birth mother, sometimes the birth father. On paper, it sounds new. No secrets. No sealed files. A child who grows up knowing both sets of parents. Sounds lovely, doesn't it? But here's the shadow: open adoption is still adoption, and adoption is still loss. The primal wound doesn't close just because you get a birthday card from a birth relative. Grief and gratitude can - and do - coexist in the same body, spinning the child in a whirl of mixed loyalties.

Open adoptions are rarely as open as they appear. They exist on a spectrum, and most are what we'd call semi-open: mediated contact through an agency, letters that are screened, visits that become sporadic or cease entirely when the adoptive parents feel threatened. The birth mother, meanwhile, often bears the invisible burden of staying grateful and uncomplicated. She must not overstep, must not grieve too loudly, must remain a safe, sanitized figure. And the adoptee must juggle two family narratives while navigating the unspoken message that questioning the arrangement is ungrateful. That can land like a ton of bricks - you're told to feel lucky while your nervous system keeps asking: But who claimed me first? Who can I cry to without risking the loss of the other?

I've seen open adoptions that became custody battles by another name, with the child as referee. I've seen birth mothers fade away, leaving a ghost more haunting than the secretive kind because you once knew her smell, her laugh, and now she's gone again. You were never meant to be managed like a timeshare schedule. Still, research suggests some critical benefits. A longitudinal study of open adoption found that adolescents with ongoing contact exhibited fewer behavioral problems and a more integrated identity than those without. But let's be careful not to turn that finding into a demand. The presence of contact doesn’t equal the absence of pain. Openness may reduce some of the identity fog - the adoption fog that clouds so many closed-record adoptees - but it doesn't cure the existential dislocation of adoption itself.

Joe Soll, in Adoption Healing... a path to recovery (paid link), emphasizes that all adoptees, open or closed, must eventually mourn the loss of the original mother - not just the relationship, but the experience of being kept, nourished, unsevered. That mourning doesn't go away because you’ve her phone number. In fact, sometimes it's harder. You can see her, talk to her, and simultaneously feel the infinite distance your adoption created. Like pressing your face against the glass of a bakery where you once knew every recipe.

The Body Keeps the Score, Regardless of the File

Across the dozens of articles we've published on this site, one pattern emerges: adoptees often feel caught between the story they were told and the truth their bodies hold. It doesn't matter if the adoption was open or closed - the body remembers the original separation. The nervous system encodes the loss, often before language. So when we talk about adoptee identity, we're not just talking about paperwork. We're talking about a biological rupture that echoes in panic attacks, attachment chaos, the eerie certainty that love is always conditional. Closed adoption amplifies this with a void of information; open adoption can sometimes magnify it with the maddening nearness of what was lost.

Both systems share a dangerous premise: that the adoptee can be molded to fit the needs of adults. In closed adoption, the need was for a completely fresh start, a child with no past. In open adoption, the need is often for a managed kinship that never disrupts the primacy of the adoptive family. Either way, the central question - what does this child truly need to be whole? - gets outsourced to lawyers, social workers, sometimes to a birth mother who is herself in crisis. You, the adoptee, become a secondary character in your own origin myth.

That said, I want to name something fierce: the secrecy of closed adoption is a particular kind of cruelty. It tells you, from the very first breath, that you’re not to be trusted with your own life. It turns a birth certificate, a document every non-adopted person takes for granted, into a lie. And you spend years excavating that lie while everyone around you insists it's nothing, told to be grateful for what you’ve. That compliance training runs deep. It can take a decade of therapy just to feel entitled to look for your own reflection.

Open adoption, on the surface, seems to counter this. But as Nancy Verrier explores in Coming Home to Self: The Adopted Child Grows Up (paid link), even with contact, the adoptee can remain stuck in a dissociated state, unable to fully claim either world. The challenge isn’t just access - it's integration. It's giving yourself permission to feel the rage and the love in the same breath, without tidying up the contradictions for someone else's comfort.

What Neither System Can Fix (and Where Hope Lives)

If you're reading this, you might be caught in a familiar loop: If only my adoption had been open, I wouldn't feel so broken. Or, if only I'd had the clean break of closed records, I wouldn't be entangled in this painful tug-of-war. I want to gently drop a truth bomb: both stories are a form of cosmic abandonment, and neither one is your fault. The question isn't which system is better - it's how you can reclaim authority over your own narrative, regardless of the structure that held you.

That reclamation often begins with naming what was actually taken. Not a narrative opportunity, but a human right: the right to know your own story. The right to decide how you relate to both biological and adoptive kin. The right to grieve in a way that doesn't have to be pretty or productive. This is where the tender guide in me steps forward. Beautiful soul, your desperation to know isn’t pathology. It’s sanity. The ache is proof of your innate wholeness, a compass pointing toward integration.

Practical steps: Get your original birth certificate if you can. If you can't, consider joining a legislative advocacy group fighting to unseal records for all adoptees. DNA tests can be a lifeline, though approach them with a buddy and a good therapist because the results can be a roller coaster. Build a circle of fellow adoptees who don't demand a neat resolution. Read the thinkers who refuse to flinch - Lifton, Verrier, Soll, Brodzinsky, and others who treat adoption as a lifelong identity voyage, not a one-time event. The path isn’t about choosing a door but about building your own doorway, wide enough for all your truth.

A Devotional for the Journey

May your rage be holy, your tears a river that carries you home. May you hold your original name - whether you know it or not - like a secret prayer on your tongue. And may you know, in your bones, that you were never meant to be a locked file or a managed contact schedule. You were meant to be a whole human being, with a story that belongs to no one but you. The system failed you. The system continues to fail you. But you? You’re not a problem to be solved. You’re a living question, and every fierce, tender step you take toward the answer is an act of liberation.

As you walk, I'll walk with you. We'll call out the lies and make space for the grief. And maybe, just maybe, we'll find that the door was always inside you - and you’ve the key.

Reviewed by The Oracle Lover, intuitive teacher with 20+ years of practice. Last updated 2026-05-01.

In our experience on this site, the stories from closed adoptions often carry a heavier layer of phantom grief, but open adoption has its own quiet desperation. We’re here for all of it.
articles.meta_description: Closed adoption seals the past, open adoption cracks a window, but neither guarantees the adoptee’s wholeness. Explore identity, the primal wound, and the search for truth.

articles.author: The Oracle Lover

articles.title: Adoptee Rage: Where the Anger Actually Comes From
articles.body:
Adoptee rage isn’t a character flaw but a primal, preverbal response to early separation and loss. It lives in the body long before the mind can name it. Understanding its roots is the first step toward freeing its hold on your life.

TL;DR

Adoptee rage is messy, fierce, and often hidden behind a mask of compliance. It's a somatic echo of the original break, not a personal failing. This article traces its origins, names its disguises, and offers companionship on the long road home.

You were a model child. Good grades. Polite. Never made a fuss. But inside, a fire raged that you couldn't explain. Maybe you still feel it, a sudden flash of fury at the grocery store, a simmering resentment that surprises you, or an explosive outburst that leaves you ashamed. Here's the thing: that anger isn’t your fault. It's not some moral defect or proof that you're broken. It's the body remembering what the mind can't.

The Rage That Has No Name

Anger in adoptees often feels like a monster with no face. It arrives without warning, disproportionate to the trigger. You might snap at a partner over a minor slight, or feel a wave of heat when someone asks an innocent question about your "real" parents. In our experience writing about adoptee experience, primal wound, identity, reunion, transracial adoption, late-discovery adoptees, we've seen this pattern again and again: a volcano beneath a calm surface.

This isn't ordinary irritation. It's a deep, physiological storm. Betty Jean Lifton, in Journey of the Adopted Self: A Quest for Wholeness (paid link), calls it "the frozen grief that thaws as rage." And she's right. When the original loss, the separation from your birth mother, happened before words, the body stored the violation. No narrative. Just surviving. So later, anything that brushes against that old wound can trigger a fight response.

Think about it: you were once in a womb, knowing nothing but that heartbeat. Then, suddenly, you were in too-bright lights and unfamiliar hands. That terror had no relief. As an infant, you couldn't protest or flee. You could only freeze or dissociate. Now, decades later, your nervous system still holds that memory. And when something pokes it, boom.

It Starts Before Language

The primal wound isn’t a metaphor. It's a biological event. Neuroscientists like Ruth Lanius and Bessel van der Kolk have shown how early relational trauma shapes the developing brain. When an infant is separated from the primary caregiver, the stress response system goes haywire. Cortisol floods. The amygdala grows hypervigilant. The right orbitofrontal cortex, which helps regulate emotion, doesn't get the attunement it needs.

You can't remember that day. But your cells do. That's why, even as an adult, you might feel a nameless sense of danger, a hair-trigger temper. It's not about what's happening now. It's about what happened then, stuck on loop. As The Primal Wound: Understanding the Adopted Child (paid link) by Nancy Newton Verrier explains, the original separation is experienced as an existential threat. And the natural response to a threat? Fight, flight, freeze, or fawn.

Many adoptees default to fawn, people-pleasing, hypervigilance, always smiling. But that fight response doesn't disappear. It goes underground, popping out sideways. Road rage. Chronic frustration. Passive-aggressive digs. Or it turns inward, becoming self-hatred, self-harm, or an inner critic that never shuts up.

The Body as a Storage Unit

Your body isn’t a passive container. It's a living library of every hurt and joy. The adoptee's body often holds tension in specific places: the jaw, the shoulders, the gut. That's not just stress, it's bracing. Bracing against a threat that's long gone but never resolved.

When I see a client whose jaw is wired tight or who punches the steering wheel over a trivial delay, I don't ask, "Why are you so angry?" I ask, "What are you protecting?" Because rage, at its core, is a boundary-setter. It rises to say, "This isn’t okay." In the adoptee, that boundary was obliterated before you could speak. So the anger keeps trying to build a wall that was never allowed.

Here's a truth: you might have been told your anger is too much. Your adoptive parents may have shamed it out of you. "Be grateful." "Look how lucky you’re." But that's a lie. Gratitude and grief can coexist. And the body will scream until it's heard.

When the Fog Lifts

Many adoptees describe waking up from a "fog", a state of denial where they believed their adoption was a perfectly happy story. The fog is a survival mechanism. But when it lifts, the rage can be staggering. Suddenly, you feel the enormity of what was lost: a lineage, a mother's arms, a mirror of your own face.

That's not a breakdown. It's a breakthrough. Though it feels like chaos, it's your psyche finally allowing the truth to surface. In Coming Home to Self: The Adopted Child Grows Up (paid link), Nancy Verrier describes this awakening as a necessary passage. The anger isn’t the enemy; it's the gatekeeper to deeper sorrow.

But without skill, that gatekeeper can take over. You might pick fights with your spouse. You might rage at the adoption industry, at your birth parents, at your adoptive parents, at yourself. All of it’s valid. And yet, unprocessed, it can burn everything down.

The Unwelcome Guest at the Reunion Table

When adoptees search for and find birth family, the fantasy is a Hallmark ending. Rarely the case. Instead, reunion often ignites the fiercest rage. At the birth mother who gave you up. At the sister who wasn't adopted. At the doctor who signed the papers. At a system that commodified your body.

You might feel jealous, possessive, furious. You might lash out over small things. This is normal. Reunion stirs the original trauma. The person you longed for is now real, and that means all the buried feelings come roaring back. The rage is about the past, not the present. It's about what should have been.

Does that land? Because you’ve to see this without shame. Many adoptees abandon reunion because the anger is too painful. But avoiding it only deepens the grove. To heal, you need to let it flow, safely.

Rage as a Gateway to Grief

Anger is often called a secondary emotion. It protects something more vulnerable. In the adopted self, that vulnerability is an ocean of unwept tears. The primal wound is a grief wound. Loss of the mother. Loss of identity. Loss of trust in the world.

Therapeutic modalities like Somatic Experiencing, EMDR, and Internal Family Systems are excellent for unwinding this knot. But you don't need a fancy acronym to start. You just need one moment of turning toward the rage with curiosity, not judgment. Where do you feel it in your body? What happens if you breathe into it? The heat might shift into trembling, then into tears. That's the grief underneath.

According to a study by early life stress research, interventions that include body awareness significantly reduce symptoms of trauma. So when you sit with the anger, you're not indulging; you're regulating. You're teaching your nervous system that the threat is over.

Sitting with the Anger Without Burning Down the House

So, what do you do with all this fire? First, stop shaming yourself. The rage exists because you survived something that could have killed you. It's a sign of your life force. But it needs channeling, not suppressing.

Try this: next time fury hits, don't act out. Don't shove it down either. Get alone. Pound a pillow. Scream into a towel. Scribble curses on a page. Move your body, run, shake, dance like a wild thing. Let the energy move. Then, when the storm quiets, ask the sensation, "What do you need?" The answer might be something simple: rest. A boundary. A story told aloud.

Adoption Healing... a path to recovery (paid link) by Joe Soll offers practical exercises for releasing stored anger. It's not about forgiveness right away. It's about discharge. You can't skip this step.

Over the years I've seen countless adoptees try to bypass their anger with spiritual platitudes. "Everything happens for a reason." "I'm choosing love over fear." That's spiritual bypassing, and it only drives the dragon deeper. Honor the dragon. Let it speak. Then you can invite it to rest.

When the Rage Is Directed at You

Perhaps the most insidious form of adoptee anger is self-directed. You hate yourself for being too much or not enough. You berate your body, your emotions, your very existence. This is the introjected voice of abandonment, the belief that if you had been better, you would have been kept.

It's a lie. But it lives deep in the cells. To unwind it, you need a counter-voice. Not affirmations alone, but a fierce inner protector that says, "I did the best a tiny human could do. I deserved safety, and it was stolen. I won’t punish myself for that."

This is where therapy, support groups, or a trusted journal practice become lifelines. The fog lifted, so the truth is visible: you’re not the problem. The problem was a severing that should never have happened. Your rage is the sound of your soul crying, "No more."

Think about it: if a child you loved was wailing in pain, would you tell them to shut up and be grateful? Never. So why do that to your inner child? The anger is a flare gun. It's asking you to rescue yourself.

A Love Note to the Enraged Adoptee

Beautiful soul, I see you. The one who punches the bed at 3 a.m. The one who wants to scream at every well-meaning aunt who asks about your "real" parents. The one whose anger has cost relationships and jobs. You’re not alone, and you’re not broken. You’re a warrior who has been fighting a battle most can’t see.

Your rage is holy. It rose from a place of unimaginable loss and kept you alive. But you don't have to live at war forever. Bit by bit, breath by breath, you can bring that exiled fury into the light. You don't need to fix it overnight. Just start by not running.

One laugh per article, remember? So here's the irreverent mystic in me: next time you're at a family gathering and Aunt Karen hints you should be more grateful, imagine your anger as a small, grumpy dragon sitting on your shoulder. It might hiccup fire, but it's also kinda cute. You can pat its head and say, "Not now, little one. We'll talk later." Then sneak off to the bathroom and let it roar into a wad of toilet paper. Problem solved.

But in all seriousness: your anger isn’t a liability. It's a messenger. And when you finally hear what it has to say, you'll discover something striking beneath it: an immense capacity for love, resilience, and truth. You deserve to meet that part of yourself.

The journey from rage to wholeness isn’t linear. It's a spiral, a dance. You'll get triggered again, and you'll forget everything you learned. That's okay. Come back. Breathe. The path is right here, always.

Now go. Turn off the screen. Feel your feet on the ground. Put a hand on your heart and say, "I’m allowed to be angry. I’m allowed to grieve. And I’m allowed to feel peace, too." That permission is the first miracle.

Reviewed by The Oracle Lover, spiritual guide and longtime student of non-dual traditions. Last updated 2026-05-01.

After years of working with seekers on the raw edges of identity and reunion, I've learned that the body always tells the truth. May this article meet you exactly where you’re.
articles.meta_description: Adoptee rage isn't random anger,it's a primal response to early separation. Discover where it lives in your body and how to begin softening its grip.

articles.title: The Fantasy Parent vs the Real Parent: Holding Both
articles.body:
TL;DR

Reunion crash-lands you into the gap between the parent you dreamed and the parent who stands before you. That gap isn’t a failure, it’s the birthplace of adult wholeness. You can love the real while grieving the fantasy without betraying either.

You found them. After years of searching, of scrolling through old records, of DNA kits and late-night internet rabbit holes, there they’re. The face that resembles yours. The voice on the phone, shaky and strange. And suddenly you realize you’ve not one parent but two: the fantasy parent you carried in your bones your whole life, and the real human being now breathing into your ear. The collision is almost unbearable. It’s also, I promise you, holy ground.

I’m The Oracle Lover, and I’ve been holding space for adoptees at this crossroads for over two decades. I’ve seen the trembling, the rage, the ecstasy, the numb disbelief. I’ve held hands with late-discovery adoptees who felt like their entire life story just caught fire. I’ve watched transracial adoptees work through the surreal double-take of a birth parent who doesn’t look like the internalized mirror they’d imagined. Across the dozens of articles I’ve published on this site, I keep returning to one truth: reunion isn’t a single event, it’s a lifelong initiation into holding paradox.

Here’s the thing: you’re not broken for loving a ghost. You’re not foolish for missing someone who never existed. The fantasy parent was a survival masterpiece, woven by a child’s mind to keep hope alive in the dark. And the real parent is a flawed, complex, often traumatized person who may or may not be capable of meeting your needs. Holding both is the work. Let’s name it together.

The Fantasy Parent: A Necessary Illusion

Think of the dozens, maybe hundreds, of tiny moments when you conjured them. In the school lunch line, you wondered if your birth mother packed carrots like the other moms. At night, you invented a father who would have read you stories and never let you feel alone. The fantasy parent was your original companion, your secret protector. And you built them without a blueprint, just the raw ache of missing.

Honestly, that longing gets pathologized. But it makes so much sense. As children, we can’t hold the full weight of abandonment without some soothing narrative. Psychologists talk about splitting, but adoptees perform a magnificent internal surgery: we split the absent parent into a shimmering ideal so we could love them (and by extension, love ourselves). The Primal Wound (paid link) by Nancy Verrier maps this exquisitely. Verrier explains that the baby records the loss at a cellular level and then spends a lifetime searching for wholeness, often projecting that search onto the fantasy of reunion.

This fantasy isn’t childish stupidity. It’s a spiritual technology. It kept your heart soft when the world told adoptees to be grateful and shut up. It preserved a thread of belonging when every document told you “origin unknown.” It allowed you to survive a system that, frankly, often treats infants like interchangeable parts. So before we rush to “let go” of the fantasy, let’s bow to it. Let’s thank it for the lifeline it was.

And then let’s admit that the fantasy parent never needed to be real. They functioned as a placeholder for your own unclaimed wholeness. That’s the Jungian twist: the King or Queen you sought is, ultimately, a lost aspect of your Self. But that’s a deeper dive for another day. For now, just notice that the fantasy parent lived as a felt sense in your body, a warmth in your sternum, a whisper in your ear. They were real to you. And that matters.

The Real Parent: The Crash of Incarnation

Then you meet the person. Maybe they’re kind but distant. Maybe they’re needy and overwhelming. Maybe they smell like cigarettes and say all the wrong things. The shock can feel like betrayal. “You’re not who I dreamed,” your heart screams. And immediately, shame: “Why can’t I just be grateful I found them?”

Look, I’m going to be fierce here. The real parent is a human being with their own primal wound. Unless you were surrendered under truly rare circumstances, your birth parent was likely in deep distress. They may have been coerced, shamed, impoverished, or themselves abandoned. When you meet them, you meet their trauma first. That’s not an excuse for harmful behavior, but it’s context. Over the years I’ve seen adoptees bend themselves into pretzels trying to “understand” or “forgive” prematurely. I’ve seen adoptees swallow their own disgust to keep the peace, then crash into autoimmune flares and depression. Your body won’t be fooled.

Here’s where we name something hard: sometimes the real parent is genuinely unsafe. Sometimes they’re narcissistic, or still using substances, or incapable of seeing you as a separate adult. In Reunion Trauma: When Finding Your Birth Family Isn't the Happy Ending, I unpack the dark side we rarely talk about. The real parent can re-wound you, not because you were too hopeful, but because they’re unhealed. And you get to have boundaries. You get to step back. You get to grieve.

But sometimes the real parent is just… ordinary. A tired woman who works at a grocery store. A quiet man who fishes on weekends. And the ordinariness is its own gut punch. You imagined a hero, a sage, a mirror. Instead, you got a stranger who doesn’t even like the same music. The disappointment is real and it deserves honor. It doesn’t mean you’re shallow. It means you held a myth in your body for decades, and now the myth has a body, and the body farts and forgets your birthday. That disenchantment is a rite of passage, not a failure of love.

Holding Both: The Nondual Embrace

So how do you hold both? How do you love the woman who handed you the stuffed rabbit in your dream and also the woman who, when you finally held her, smelled like mothballs and said she “did the right thing” with a flat smile? You do it by refusing to split. You do it by staying in the fire of “and.”

This is the mystic’s path. I mean that. Nondual traditions teach us that reality is whole, and our suffering comes from slicing it into this-or-that. The fantasy parent isn’t the enemy of the real parent. They’re two faces of the same aching love story. You can say, “I loved you before I met you, and I’m angry at you now. You saved my life at birth and you just hurt me with your dismissive comment. I hold the dream-version close, and I will never let anyone shame me for it, even as I learn to see you clearly.”

Integration is messy. It looks like waking up at 3 a.m. with your jaw clenched and tears streaming, remembering a tender moment from the reunion and then a sharp betrayal in the same breath. It looks like writing a letter to the fantasy parent, then one to the real parent, then a third letter that speaks to them as one. I guide so many adoptees through this right now. And I always recommend Journey of the Adopted Self (paid link) by Betty Jean Lifton as a companion text. Lifton calls the adopted child an “orphan in both worlds,” always bridging the gap between the imagined and the actual. Her work will crack you open in the best way.

Does that land? I want you to really sit with the permission to love a fantasy. It’s not regressive. It’s honoring the deepest wisdom of your child-self. Meanwhile, you’re also allowed to set fierce limits with the real person. You’re allowed to say, “I can’t talk every week, but I will send birthday cards.” Or, “I need you to listen without giving advice right now.” The real parent doesn’t get to erase the fantasy parent, and the fantasy parent doesn’t get to blind you to reality.

The Body Remembers: Somatics and the Primal Wound

You can’t think your way into holding both. This is body work. The first time you spoke on the phone with your birth mother, your nervous system likely screamed “mama!” and “danger!” simultaneously. That’s the primal wound, a preverbal imprint of separation and longing. It lives in your fascia, your gut, your startle reflex. When you talk about “holding both,” you’re asking your animal body to do something counterintuitive: stay present with contradictory sensations without collapsing into freeze.

I often teach clients a simple practice: place one hand on your chest and one on your belly. Breathe. Then invite the fantasy parent to sit beside you (yes, literally imagine them). Notice any warmth or ache. Then invite the real parent to the other side. Notice any heat, numbness, rage. Stay with both. This isn’t about forcing a resolution. It’s about expanding your container. Over time, the body learns it can hold the love picture and the hurt picture in the same frame without shattering. Adoption Healing... a path to recovery (paid link) by Joe Soll offers more somatic and grief-informed tools for this exact process.

And let’s not forget rage. The primal wound carries a molten fury at being separated from the source. That fury often attaches to the real parent because they’re finally here, a target. But the real parent may not be the sole cause of your suffering. In Birth Mother Grief: The Other Side of the Adoption Story, I explore how systemic forces and coercive adoption practices created this rift. Your anger is sacred, but it’s bigger than one person. When you can feel the anger while also sensing the fantasy parent holding your hand, something shifts. The anger metabolizes into sorrow, and sorrow into tenderness. Not forgiveness yet, tenderness. That’s enough.

The Adoptive Parent’s Blind Spot (and Yours)

This is the moment I ask you to consider the third player: your adoptive parents. They, too, may have had fantasies about the birth parents, often fear-based or competitive ones. You may have internalized those. When you meet the real parent, you’re not just dealing with your own split; you’re often navigating your adoptive family’s unspoken anxieties. I cover this in depth in The Adoptive Parent’s Blind Spot: What Your Parents Can’t See. Your adoptive mom might hear “I found my real mom” and feel a stab of insecurity. Your adoptive dad might dismiss the whole endeavor because he’s terrified of losing you. All of it complicates your ability to hold both the fantasy and the real.

Know what I mean? You can’t hold both in a vacuum. The system is designed to keep the triad fractured, each side clutching its own version of the truth. So when you start integrating, you’re actually a new. You’re disrupting the adoption fantasy that everyone else clings to (the grateful orphan narrative, the selfless birth mother trope). By holding both internally, you heal not just yourself but the lineage. That’s not airy-fairy. That’s epigenetic repair.

A recent study on long-term psychological outcomes in adoption reunion underscores the complexity: Mental health in adult adoptees after reunion (NIH, 2021) found that reunion can both trigger deep distress and help post-traumatic growth. In other words, the crash of fantasy and reality can break you open or break you down, depending on the support you’ve. That’s why I’m so damn loud about doing this work in community, with fierce guides, and with all the self-compassion you can muster.

The Gift of Disillusionment

There’s a strange grace in disillusionment. When the fantasy parent finally steps aside, you see the real human with sober eyes. And in that clear seeing, an adult-to-adult connection becomes possible. It may not be the deep, nurturing bond you craved. It may be a friendship built on mutual respect and shared DNA. It may be a once-a-year phone call that you initiate with a deep breath. Or it may be a door you close quietly, with love, because the real person can’t offer safe relationship.

Right?! I love the word “disillusionment” because it literally means “free from illusion.” It’s not a defeat. It’s an awakening. The mystic knows that the face of the Beloved is everywhere and in no single face. When you release the fantasy parent from the job of saving you, you reclaim your own soul. And then you get to decide what you want from the real parent. You’re no longer a child begging for a reflection. You’re an adult who can say, “I see you. I carry the dream of you in my heart, and I also see your limits. I choose to relate from this truth.”

Something hilarious happens, too. The moment you stop needing the real parent to match the fantasy, they often relax. They stop trying to perform or defend. You might discover a dark, shared sense of humor. You might learn that you both hate cilantro. These small, mundane connections are the blossoms that grow in the shattered soil of the myth. They’re not the grand romance you imagined, but they’re real, and they can nourish in surprising ways.

A Daily Practice for the Split Heart

Let me give you something concrete. Every morning, while your coffee is brewing, speak to them both. Out loud if you dare.

To the fantasy parent: “I made you from longing. You gave me hope when I had none. I thank you. I release you from having to be real.”

To the real parent: “You’re a mystery to me. I hold space for your wounds. I also hold my own. May our connection be honest, if it’s to be.”

This isn’t a chant for closure. It’s an ongoing attunement. Some days you’ll weep. Some days you’ll feel ridiculous. Keep going. The split heart integrates not by erasing one side, but by letting them coexist as two witnesses to your life. Coming Home to Self (paid link) by Nancy Verrier devotes entire chapters to this post-reunion identity work. Verrier’s voice is the grandmother you needed, stern and endlessly tender.

And please, for the love of all that’s holy, don’t do this alone. Find an adoption-competent therapist. Join an adoptee support group. In The Search: How to Find Your Birth Parents (And Whether You Should), I talk about preparing your nervous system before you even click “send” on that first message. If you’re already in reunion, it’s not too late to fortify yourself. The integration of fantasy and real is a hero’s journey, and every hero needs allies.

Today is May 1, 2026. On this very morning, somewhere in the world, an adoptee is staring at a photograph of a stranger’s face that feels like home. Another is deleting a contact from her phone with shaking fingers. Another is laughing through tears because her birth mother just said, “I always hated peas too.” All of them are holding both. And so are you, Beautiful Soul. So are you.

Reviewed by The Oracle Lover, intuitive teacher with 20+ years of practice. Last updated 2026-05-01.

Across the dozens of articles I’ve written here on adoptee experience, I return again and again to the aching beauty of holding contradiction. This one is for the 3 a.m. griever who still loves the dream. I see you.
articles.meta_description: Reunion shatters the dream parent we carried inside. Learn to hold the grief and the love for both the fantasy and the real, without losing yourself.
